title: SEGMENT 10 — The limitations of bisulfite — DNA damage, 5mC versus 5hmC, and the hidden trouble

lines.text: SEGMENT 10 — The limitations of bisulfite — DNA damage, 5mC versus 5hmC, and the hidden trouble
Welcome back.
I hope you stretched your legs and got some coffee.

We've spent the first nine segments building up the bisulfite-sequencing universe — the chemistry, the genomic-scale assays, the analytical conventions — and now I want to spend the next several segments dismantling some of the assumptions and meeting the next generation of methods that are slowly displacing the bisulfite era.
Let me lay out, in detail, the three big limitations of bisulfite sequencing.
Understanding these limitations is essential, because every modern alternative — oxBS, TAPS, EM-seq, nanopore methylation calling — is designed to solve one or more of them.
If you don't see why bisulfite is limited, you won't appreciate why anyone bothered to replace it.
Limitation one: DNA damage.
I mentioned this in Segment 4, but let me put hard numbers on it.
A typical sodium-bisulfite reaction proceeds at pH 5 and 50 to 70 degrees Celsius for several hours.
Under these conditions, DNA suffers extensive damage.
The phosphodiester backbone hydrolyzes, generating single-strand and eventually double-strand breaks.
Purines depurinate at elevated rates, leaving abasic sites that further destabilize the strand.
The cumulative effect is that something like 70 to 90 percent of the starting DNA mass is destroyed during bisulfite conversion.
The DNA that survives is fragmented down to a few hundred base pairs in length and is enriched for shorter fragments.
For a research project with milligrams of DNA from a tissue culture flask, that loss is annoying but manageable.
For a precious clinical sample — say, a 20-microliter plasma draw from a cancer patient, containing maybe a few nanograms of cell-free DNA — that loss is catastrophic.

You start with a tiny amount of input; you lose 80 percent of it to bisulfite damage; you end up with a marginal library, low complexity, full of PCR duplicates because the unique molecule count is so low.

For applications where input is precious — clinical liquid biopsy, single-cell methylation, formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded tumor samples, ancient DNA — bisulfite damage is the single biggest barrier to getting good data.
Limitation two: bisulfite cannot distinguish 5mC from 5hmC.
Remember from Segment 2 that 5-hydroxymethylcytosine is a distinct epigenetic mark, with its own biology, accumulating to several percent of total cytosines in some tissues.
When you bisulfite-treat DNA, both 5mC and 5hmC fail to convert.
They both read as C in the sequencing output.
There is no way, from a standard bisulfite experiment, to tell them apart.
Every methylation call you make is actually a "5mC plus 5hmC" call, with the contribution of 5hmC silently mixed in.
For most tissues, where 5hmC is rare, this conflation barely matters — the 5mC signal dominates, and treating bisulfite data as a 5mC readout is approximately correct.

But for tissues where 5hmC is abundant — particularly the brain, where 5hmC can make up 1 to 2 percent of all cytosines and is concentrated in active gene bodies — the conflation can produce systematically misleading results.
A region that looks "methylated" in bisulfite data might actually be hydroxymethylated, with completely different biological implications.
The Lister and Ecker groups and others demonstrated this clearly in early-2010s papers comparing bisulfite data to specialized 5hmC assays in brain tissue.
The bisulfite map of brain methylation is, in important regions, actually a map of bisulfite-resistant marks of both flavors, and you can't tell which is which.
Limitation three: three-base alignment, low information content, and PCR bias.
The post-bisulfite sequence has essentially no unmethylated Cs — they've all been converted to Ts.
The effective alphabet of the read drops from four letters to three.
Reads become harder to align uniquely.
About 20 to 30 percent of reads in a typical WGBS experiment fail to map uniquely and are discarded.
The library prep also requires extensive PCR amplification after bisulfite conversion, both because the input is low and because the bisulfite-damaged DNA needs to be expanded to a sequenceable mass.
PCR amplification introduces sequence-context biases and produces duplicate reads that have to be filtered out, further reducing the effective coverage.
The three-base problem also affects the design of methylation arrays, where probes have to be designed around the bisulfite-converted sequence and where probe specificity is harder to ensure.
Some array probes are cross-reactive, binding multiple genomic locations because the three-base converted sequences happen to match in more than one place.
Lists of cross-reactive probes are maintained for the Illumina arrays, and standard quality control involves dropping those probes from the analysis.

So bisulfite, the workhorse chemistry of the methylation field for nearly thirty years, has three serious limitations: it damages DNA, it conflates 5mC and 5hmC, and it compresses the sequencing alphabet in ways that hurt alignment and library quality.
Each of these limitations has motivated a separate methodological response, and the next several segments are about those responses.
The first response, historically, was a clever chemical addition to bisulfite that resolves the 5mC versus 5hmC ambiguity without abandoning bisulfite itself.
Let's meet it.
